A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus.
Neptune has the strongest winds of any planet in the solar system, reaching up to 2,100 km/h.
Jupiter's Great Red Spot is a storm larger than Earth that has been raging for hundreds of years.
Saturn is so light it would float in water — its density is less than water's.
The Sun makes up 99.86% of the total mass of the solar system.
Mars has the tallest mountain in the solar system — Olympus Mons at 21.9 km high.
There are more stars in the universe than grains of sand on all of Earth's beaches.
A year on Mercury is just 88 Earth days.
Uranus rotates on its side, with an axial tilt of 98 degrees.
Earth's moon is the fifth largest moon in the solar system.
Venus is the hottest planet in the solar system with surface temperatures reaching 462°C.
Jupiter has at least 95 known moons.
Saturn has 146 known moons — the most of any planet in the solar system.
One day on Earth is 24 hours, but one day on Jupiter is only about 10 hours.
The solar system is about 4.6 billion years old.
Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006.
The closest star to Earth (after the Sun) is Proxima Centauri, about 4.24 light-years away.
Earth's atmosphere is composed of about 78% nitrogen and 21% oxygen.
Mercury has no atmosphere and no moons.
The rings of Saturn are made mostly of ice and rock particles.
